title: Myasthenic Crisis
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Neurology, Emergency Medicine, Critical Care
objectives: Recognize myasthenic crisis and distinguish from cholinergic crisis
Apply rapid treatment with IVIG or plasmapheresis

vignette.text:
A 52-year-old woman with known myasthenia gravis presents with worsening dyspnea, dysphagia, and weakness over 3 days. She recently completed a course of azithromycin for pneumonia. She is now having difficulty speaking and is using accessory muscles.

Past Medical History: Myasthenia gravis (AChR antibody positive), s/p thymectomy

Medications: Pyridostigmine 60mg QID, prednisone 20mg daily

physicalExam.neuro: Ptosis bilateral, weak neck flexion, nasal speech, symmetric proximal weakness 3/5

questions.stem: This patient has worsening MG symptoms with respiratory compromise.
questions.question: What defines myasthenic crisis?

options.text: MYASTHENIC CRISIS: DEFINITION: Life-threatening weakness requiring mechanical ventilation or airway protection; OR imminent respiratory failure; CLINICAL FEATURES: Rapidly progressive weakness; Bulbar symptoms: dysphagia, dysarthria, weak cough; Respiratory: dyspnea, tachypnea, accessory muscle use; FVC <15-20 mL/kg or NIF weaker than -30 cmH2O; TRIGGERS: Infection (most common - like this patient's pneumonia); Medications (aminoglycosides, macrolides, fluoroquinolones, beta-blockers, Mg); Surgery; Pregnancy/postpartum; Tapering immunosuppression; DISTINGUISH FROM CHOLINERGIC CRISIS: Excess acetylcholinesterase inhibitors; SLUDGE symptoms: Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI distress, Emesis; Miosis, bradycardia, fasciculations
Medications never trigger crisis
Any MG exacerbation is a crisis
Respiratory status is not relevant to definition

questions.explanation: Myasthenic Crisis: Life-threatening weakness requiring ventilation or with imminent respiratory failure. Features: progressive weakness, bulbar symptoms, respiratory distress. TRIGGERS: Infection (most common), medications (aminoglycosides, macrolides like azithromycin, fluoroquinolones, beta-blockers), surgery, pregnancy. Distinguish from cholinergic crisis (excess pyridostigmine) which has SLUDGE symptoms, miosis, bradycardia.

sources.text: StatPearls: Myasthenia Gravis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: When and how should you manage the airway?

options.text: AIRWAY MANAGEMENT IN MG CRISIS: INTUBATION CRITERIA: FVC <15 mL/kg (or <1 L); NIF weaker than -20 to -25 cmH2O; Declining FVC (>30% drop); Severe bulbar weakness with aspiration; Inability to clear secretions; Rising CO2 or respiratory fatigue; THIS PATIENT: FVC 0.8L = critical, needs intubation; INTUBATION CONSIDERATIONS: Use non-depolarizing NMB (rocuronium) - may need lower dose; AVOID succinylcholine (prolonged paralysis due to reduced cholinesterase); Consider awake intubation if possible; Have difficult airway equipment ready; POST-INTUBATION: Hold pyridostigmine initially (reduces secretions); Can resume when improving; BiPAP may bridge if mild and improving
Wait for CO2 retention to intubate
Succinylcholine is preferred for RSI
FVC 0.8 L is adequate

questions.explanation: Airway in MG Crisis: Intubate for FVC <15 mL/kg (or <1L), NIF weaker than -20-25, declining FVC, severe bulbar weakness, aspiration risk. This patient with FVC 0.8L needs intubation. Use non-depolarizing NMB (rocuronium) at reduced dose. AVOID succinylcholine (prolonged paralysis). Hold pyridostigmine initially (reduces secretions). Consider BiPAP only as bridge in mild cases.

sources.text: StatPearls: Myasthenia Gravis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What is the treatment for myasthenic crisis?

options.text: High-dose steroids are first-line for crisis
Continue pyridostigmine at high doses
IVIG and PLEX should be combined

MG CRISIS TREATMENT: IMMUNOTHERAPY (rapid acting): PLASMAPHERESIS: 5 exchanges over 10-14 days; Removes circulating antibodies; Faster onset than IVIG; Often preferred in crisis; OR IVIG: 2 g/kg divided over 2-5 days; Equally effective long-term; May be easier to administer; CORTICOSTEROIDS: Chronic management, not acute crisis; May cause initial worsening ('steroid dip'); Start or increase after crisis stabilizes; HOLD PYRIDOSTIGMINE: Initially in intubated patients; Reduces secretions; Resume when extubated; TREAT TRIGGER: Antibiotics for infection (avoid harmful ones); Remove offending medication; LONG-TERM: Optimize maintenance therapy; Consider thymectomy if not done

questions.explanation: MG Crisis Treatment: PLASMAPHERESIS (5 exchanges) OR IVIG (2g/kg over 2-5 days) - equally effective. PLEX often preferred for faster onset. HOLD pyridostigmine initially in intubated patients. Steroids for chronic management but may cause initial worsening - don't start in acute crisis. Treat underlying trigger. Do NOT combine PLEX and IVIG.

sources.text: StatPearls: Myasthenia Gravis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Her crisis was triggered by azithromycin.
questions.question: Which medications should be avoided in MG?

options.text: Beta-blockers are safe
Magnesium is safe in MG
All antibiotics are safe

MEDICATIONS TO AVOID IN MG: ANTIBIOTICS: Aminoglycosides (gentamicin, tobramycin); Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin); MACROLIDES (azithromycin - like this patient); Tetracyclines; CARDIOVASCULAR: Beta-blockers; Calcium channel blockers (some); Procainamide, quinidine; NEUROMUSCULAR BLOCKERS: Avoid if possible; If needed: reduced dose non-depolarizing; AVOID succinylcholine; OTHERS: Magnesium sulfate; Botulinum toxin; D-penicillamine; Quinine; Statins (rarely); SAFE ALTERNATIVES: Penicillins; Cephalosporins; Carbapenems; Vancomycin; Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole

questions.explanation: Medications to Avoid in MG: ANTIBIOTICS: Aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, macrolides (like azithromycin - this patient's trigger), tetracyclines. CARDIAC: Beta-blockers, some CCBs. OTHERS: Magnesium, botulinum toxin, D-penicillamine. SAFE antibiotics: Penicillins, cephalosporins, carbapenems, vancomycin, TMP-SMX.

sources.text: StatPearls: Myasthenia Gravis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What is the complete management?

options.text: Wait for improvement before PLEX

Complete approach: (1) RECOGNIZE CRISIS: FVC 0.8L (<15 mL/kg), bulbar symptoms, respiratory distress after macrolide antibiotic trigger; (2) INTUBATE: FVC critically low; Use rocuronium (avoid succinylcholine); (3) HOLD PYRIDOSTIGMINE (reduces secretions while intubated); (4) START PLASMAPHERESIS: 5 exchanges over 10-14 days; OR IVIG 2g/kg over 2-5 days; (5) TREAT INFECTION: Use SAFE antibiotics (cephalosporin, not fluoroquinolone/macrolide); (6) SUPPORTIVE: DVT prophylaxis, nutrition, PT; (7) RESUME pyridostigmine when extubating; (8) OPTIMIZE long-term therapy after crisis resolves; (9) EDUCATE patient on triggers and medication avoidance
Use fluoroquinolone for pneumonia
Start high-dose steroids immediately

questions.explanation: Complete Approach: Recognize crisis (FVC <15 mL/kg, bulbar symptoms, triggered by macrolide). Intubate using rocuronium. Hold pyridostigmine while intubated. Start PLEX or IVIG immediately. Treat infection with SAFE antibiotics (avoid macrolides, fluoroquinolones). Supportive care. Resume pyridostigmine when extubating. Optimize long-term therapy and educate on triggers.

sources.text: StatPearls: Myasthenia Gravis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Complete approach: (1) RECOGNIZE CRISIS: FVC 0.8L (<15 mL/kg), bulbar symptoms, respiratory distress after macrolide antibiotic trigger; (2) INTUBATE: FVC critically low; Use rocuronium (avoid succinylcholine); (3) HOLD PYRIDOSTIGMINE (reduces secretions while intubated); (4) START PLASMAPHERESIS: 5 exchanges over 10-14 days; OR IVIG 2g/kg over 2-5 days; (5) TREAT INFECTION: Use SAFE antibiotics (cephalosporin, not fluoroquinolone/macrolide); (6) SUPPORTIVE: DVT prophylaxis, nutrition, PT; (7) RESUME pyridostigmine when extubating; (8) OPTIMIZE long-term therapy after crisis resolves; (9) EDUCATE patient on triggers and medication avoidance because Complete Approach: Recognize crisis (FVC <15 mL/kg, bulbar symptoms, triggered by macrolide). Intubate using rocuronium.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected myasthenic crisis?

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in myasthenic crisis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in myasthenic crisis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in myasthenic crisis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

A.misconception: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.
A.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
A.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

C.misconception: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
C.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
C.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

D.misconception: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
D.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of myasthenic crisis. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
correct.clinicalPearl: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

A.misconception: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
A.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.
A.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Consider the differential diagnosis systematically because it best fits the clinical scenario.

C.misconception: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.
C.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.
C.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Consider the differential diagnosis systematically because it best fits the clinical scenario.

D.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.
D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Consider the differential diagnosis systematically because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: When managing myasthenic crisis, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

B.guidance: Consider why Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values is the key concept.
B.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values because it best fits the clinical scenario.

C.guidance: Consider why Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values is the key concept.

mcqs.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with optic neuritis. MRI brain shows 4 periventricular white matter lesions. CSF shows oligoclonal bands. Which criteria are met for MS diagnosis?

options.text: Neither criterion is met — optic neuritis is a clinically isolated syndrome only
options.explanation: While optic neuritis alone is a CIS, the 4 periventricular white matter lesions establish DIS. CIS + DIS constitutes significant risk for MS.

options.text: CSF oligoclonal bands alone establish the diagnosis regardless of MRI findings
options.explanation: Oligoclonal bands support the diagnosis and can substitute for DIT in certain scenarios per 2017 McDonald criteria, but they do not independently establish the diagnosis without meeting DIS.

options.text: Both DIS and DIT are met — diagnose MS now
options.explanation: DIS is met but DIT requires evidence of lesions at different time points. The initial scan alone (even with oligoclonal bands) cannot establish DIT unless both enhancing AND non-enhancing lesions are present simultaneously.

options.text: Dissemination in space (DIS) is met; need evidence of dissemination in time (DIT) for definitive diagnosis
options.explanation: Per the 2017 McDonald criteria, DIS requires ≥1 T2 lesion in ≥2 of 4 MS-typical regions (periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord). With periventricular lesions + optic neuritis (separate CNS region), DIS is met. DIT can be met by a new T2 or gadolinium-enhancing lesion on follow-up MRI, or simultaneously by both enhancing and non-enhancing lesions.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Dissemination in space (DIS) is met; need evidence of dissemination in time (DIT) for definitive diagnosis.
correct.deeperWhy: Per the 2017 McDonald criteria, DIS requires ≥1 T2 lesion in ≥2 of 4 MS-typical regions (periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord).
